sections.subject: History

entries.title: About the Course
entries.content:
This course includes the following topic(s): Canadian History: Grades 5-6, World History: Grades 4-6, Ancient History: Grades 5-6

About World History: Grades 4-6
Through the reading of history stories and biographies, students experience what life was like in different times and places. They are exposed to the everyday experiences as well as the major events from our current history cycle (1800-1900). Students will also view images, reference maps, and work on their century charts and Book of Centuries as part of their lessons.

About Ancient History: Grades 5-6
Study history stories about the ancient civilizations that lived from 100 BC to 350 AD. View images and reference atlases and maps to set the people and events in their time and place. Update History Charts and Book of Centuries as appropriate for student level.

About Canadian History: Grades 5-6
Read about Canadian history from 1800-1900 through a variety of texts: engaging narratives, history spines, primary source documents, and historical fiction. These books, which offer a range of perspectives, will immerse students in the time period and allow them to see the world through the eyes of those who lived it. Students will view images and reference atlases and maps to set the people and events in their time and place. Update History Charts and Book of Centuries as appropriate for student level.

entries.title: Placement & Combining Tips
entries.content:
World History: Grades 4-6
This course is the same for 4th-6th graders. In grade 5, students may begin their Book of Centuries.

Canadian History: Grades 5-6
This course is the same for students in grades 5 and 6. In grade 5, students may begin their Book of Centuries. It could be easily modified for students in grade 4, if combining.

entries.content:
Permission to print for non-commercial use. See Alveary group use policy to use lessons
in a group context.

LINKS: Click text or scan the QR code in the top corner of the lesson plan pages to view
online resources associated with the lessons.

Responsibility for previewing all links rests with the teacher. All links were checked at the
time of publication; however, websites change frequently and may contain objectionable
content. Please report broken links by contacting us through our website.

History: Grades 5-6 (Canada)
☐ Read the document in the Quick Links about History Timelines and Charts.

☐ We encourage you to pick a biography for each term to read in the afternoons. The Bookshelf has many recommendations.

entries.title: Term Prep & Teacher Tips
entries.content:
Canadian History: Grades 5-6
Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune is a historical novel that recounts the brave rescue of sailors from the HMS Tribune by a thirteen-year-old boy known only as "Joe Cracker" to history. The author Jacqueline Halsey imagines a backstory for him, and parents may wish to pre-read the reveal of Joe's parentage on pages 145-146 in advance to decide what is best for their particular student(s), especially those on the younger end of the age range. While the circumstances of Joe's birth and the small boat he later uses in his rescue both caused the fracturing of a family, Joe himself and the boat are what ultimately mend and heal the remaining family members. Some may choose to read the story aloud to edit on the fly if needed and to encourage discussion. Others will be fine with the content as is. We encourage all to provide an inviting space for students to ask questions and share thoughts as they process what they are reading in their books. There is always the option of substituting for Belle of Batoche from Grades 2-4 or Jeremy's War from Grades 7-8.

books.title: Commodore Perry in the Land of the Shogun
Redemption: The Church in Ancient Times
Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune

blocks.text:
• Read through the lesson and gather any materials or find any necessary links. 

• If you have not already read the book, you may want to read the selected pages in advance.

• Connect to previous lessons by asking students to recall where they left off. 

• If students have difficulty recalling, teachers might try showing them a picture from the reading, sharing something the teacher remembers, or prompting them with the first sentence of the last reading. This will often refresh their memory enough to allow them to recall.

• If students still cannot recall anything from the last reading, teachers might consider continuing anyway (with a shorter reading this time) or repeating the previous lesson, depending on the reading and the nature of the book.

• Use a picture, map, or engaging question to pique the student's interest in the day's lesson. 

• If necessary, you may talk about one or two unfamiliar words that will be crucial to understanding.

• Read the day's passage out loud. When reading to pre-readers, point to the words as you read, even if it seems like they aren't paying attention.

• Move at the student’s pace for the lesson time rather than a particular number of pages. It is vitally important that teachers not try to ‘force feed’ a student more than they can consume in one sitting.

• Students who have a difficult time sitting still or listening may benefit from a fidget of their choice, such as a piece of putty or clay or an afghan; flexible positioning, such as a wobble seat or inverted sitting; or sensory modulation, such as a weighted vest or blanket or adjustment of light, temperature, or sound.

• Have students retell the passage orally.

• Alternatively, learners may use words, pictures, Legos, dolls, etc., to process and convey ideas in the manner most natural to them.  

• Teachers may take turns to model.

• Narration is a complex skill requiring the coordination of several parts of the brain. If a student struggles to narrate in general, with a specific subject or book, or on one day but not others, then this is an opportunity to learn. Observe when they struggle with this or any other activity today. Notice facial expressions and body language; listen to what they say about why it is hard or how they feel about it. Take note of these observations for later, tell them that you see how hard they are working, and then offer whatever support they need to end on a positive note. If necessary, take a break for the day.

• Give the student an opportunity to share thoughts on the lesson and any connections made.  

• To stimulate discussion, you could ask them what they thought about the passage or ask an open-ended question.

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune
⍞ Materials: Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune; Canadian Student Atlas or map of Nova Scotia

➜ INTRO
The years 1800-1900 were a momentous century for the history of Canada. It saw wars, rebellions, large waves of immigration, new and expanded settlements, exploration to the west and north, and Canada's Confederation of 1867, with more provinces and territories joining in the decades that followed. This course uses the narrative history The Story of Canada to provide an overview of the major events of the years 1800-1900, and a historical novel, a picture book, and a graphic novel to immerse students in three distinct moments in time: life in a fishing village on Nova Scotia's Chebucto Peninsula at the turn of the century, the life of Tecumseh, a Shawnee chief and British army ally in the War of 1812, and the Confederation Conferences of 1864, where the wheels of Canada's Confederacy were set in motion.
The first book, Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune, tells the story of a thirteen-year-old boy who risked his life to save drowning sailors from the wreck of the H.M.S. Tribune, off the coast of Nova Scotia. This historical fiction novel imagines a backstory for the boy only known to history as "Joe Cracker," and it paints a detailed picture of what life was like in a small fishing village around 1800.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.1-13 "Wisps of fog" - "all about the ocean."

• Who are the characters you meet as the novel opens? How are they related to each other? What are they like? You may choose to keep a character list with some notes.

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resources: Canadian Student Atlas p.41 or any other map (including online) showing Nova Scotia
Locate Halifax and the Chebucto Peninsula. Does your map show Herring Cove?

➜ VIEW
∞ Video Link: Explore Herring Cove
Watch a video tour of Herring Cove with Senator Stan Kutcher. If you're short on time, feel free to skip 1:58-2:54. This whole video could be saved for another point in the course, but it touches on many themes of the novel, so it's definitely not a video to miss!
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER NOTE
Please read the header pages for this course that list the page numbers for the sensitive content you may wish to preview.

lessons.title: 25m World History: Grades 4-6 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Under the Hawthorn Tree

➜ INTRO
1. When you think of Ireland, what comes to mind? Perhaps the story of St. Patrick.  Ireland is often called the Emerald Isle because of its lush greenery. It is considered an enchanting place to visit.
				∞ Image Link: The Cliffs of Moher

1. However, in the 1840s tragedy struck Ireland that changed it forever. There was a terrible famine and many people either died or left the country to go to America. Today one-sixth of US citizens (43 million) identify their national background as Irish. How did this famine occur and what was it like to live in Ireland at this time? This book is a fictional account of an Irish family during that time and what they did to survive. The author is from Ireland and has sequels to this book if you want to read more. 


➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.1- beginning of ch.2 p.1-11 "The air felt" - "they reached home."

➜ VIEW
∞ Image Link:

p.13-23 "I broke my arm" - "Next job, Eli's shed."

• As you read, compare Joe's daily life to yours. For example, have you or anyone in your family ever broken a bone? How was it treated? How was Joe's treatment different? Or, what are your chores in the morning? Are they similar to or different from Joe's?

lessons.body:
Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune
⍞ Materials: Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune

➜ RECAP
What do you remember from last week's reading about the types of work Joe has to do?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.48-60 "Ten long days of tides" - "to the old shed."

• Earlier in the novel, we learned that Joe has never been to school. This was not unusual two hundred years ago, when children were often needed to work inside or outside the home to help support their families. Why do you think Joe has never been to school? How does he learn what he needs to know?

lessons.body:
Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune
⍞ Materials: Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune

➜ RECAP
What do you remember about Race Day from last week's reading?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.111-124 "Winter arrived that night." - "Frederick lagged behind."

• What has happened to the Tribune? Why won't the captain receive assistance from either other Navy ships or the local fishermen?

➜ VIEW
∞ Image Link: Capture of La Tribune June 8th 1796, Thomas Whitcombe
Look at this painting by maritime painter Thomas Whitcombe (1763-1824) of the Capture of the Tribune in 1796 by the British Navy. The Tribune was originally part of the French navy before its capture. What do you notice in the painting about these types of ships called frigates?
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER TIP
Today's reading is a bit longer than the others, so you may want to leave extra time or finish the reading in the afternoon.

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 16
lessons.body:
The War of 1812
⍞ Materials: The Story of Canada; atlas or map of Canada

➜ INTRO
In Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune, when Joe gets a tour of a Navy ship, the sailor Jacko tells him, "We's at war, you know." The British Navy had been engaged in naval conflicts for much of the eighteenth century and continued until peace was declared in 1815 after the Battle of Waterloo. One of Britain's last major battles before 1815 was the War of 1812, which involved Canada. Over the next few weeks, we will learn more about this war and why it was significant to Canada's history.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.78-81 "Britain and the United States" - "British North America would stay British."
p.92 and 94 (skip page 93) "A few years later" - "into the North."

➜ MAP WORK
Using a map of Ontario or Canada, locate Queenston Heights (near Niagara Falls), Detroit, Toronto (called York in 1812), Montreal, the St. Lawrence River, and the Great Lakes (Lake Ontario, Lake Erie, Lake Huron, Lake Michigan, and Lake Superior).

➜ VIEW
1. Spend some time looking at this engraving of The Battle of Queenston Heights. The original engraving this one is based on is attributed to Captain James Dennis, who fought in the battle. The Americans (in blue) are crossing the Niagara River to Queenston (the right side) from Lewiston (the left side).
∞ Image Link: The Battle of Queenston Heights

2. Visit Brock's Monument at Queenston Heights. You don't need to watch the whole video, but the drone footage provides an amazing view!
∞ Video Link: Canada's Most Imposing War Memorial
lessons.teacherNotes:
• IMPORTANT DATES
Tecumseh (1768-1813)
Sir Isaac Brock (1769–1812)
War of 1812 (1812)

• EXTRA HELPING 
Watch a Heritage Minute about how the Grand River warriors helped to defeat the Americans at Queenston Heights after the death of General Brock. 
∞ Video Link: Heritage Minutes: Queenston Heights

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 17
lessons.body:
Tecumseh
⍞ Materials: Tecumseh

➜ INTRO
For the next three weeks, you will learn more about the life of Tecumseh, the Shawnee chief and warrior who allied with the British against the threat of invasion from the United States during the War of 1812. Tecumseh rallied other Indigenous peoples to side with him and had a vision of the ancestral lands being restored to First Nations tribes. He was not only an ally but was a personal friend of General Isaac Brock, who commanded the British troops in Upper Canada. Tecumseh played an integral part in protecting British North America from the American army. 

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.8-21 "Tecumseh was a renowned Shawnee" - "leader of the native peoples."

• What was Tecumseh's childhood like? What were some of the most formative events in his early years?
• The illustrations in this book are very vivid. Which ones stand out to you the most?
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER NOTE
Parents/teachers may wish to preview this picture book for more sensitive readers. Tecumseh lived through turbulent times, and the story does not omit the loss and violence of battles. Note, if combining: some illustrations may upset younger children.

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 20
lessons.body:
The Métis & The Red River Settlement
⍞ Materials: The Story of Canada; map or atlas of Canada that includes Manitoba

➜ INTRO
While the War of 1812 was at its height in Upper and Lower Canada to the east, a Scottish nobleman named Thomas Douglas, 5th Earl of Selkirk, was working to establish a colony on the prairies to the west, at the intersection of the Red and Assiniboine Rivers, near what is now Winnipeg. Selkirk had received a large grant of land from the Hudson Bay Company and wanted to create a farming community to support many poor Scottish highlanders and Irish peasants, who had lost their homes and livelihoods due to various upheavals at the end of the eighteenth century. This community, known as the Red River Settlement, would come to play a large role in the history of immigrants to the prairies, the Métis peoples, the province of Manitoba, and the history of Canada as a nation.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.114-120 "In April 1806" - "cries of despair."

➜ MAPPING ACTIVITY
Look at a map or atlas of Manitoba (online is fine) and locate the Red River and Fort Douglas (present-day Winnipeg). Find the Hudson Bay on the same map so you can see the route the settlers took.
Compare this modern map to a map of the land given to Selkirk:
∞ Map Link: Map showing the Selkirk Land Grant in 1811

➜ VIEW
Learn more about elements of Métis culture, several of which are mentioned in today's reading: Métis sash, Red River cart, Red River jig, Métis flag, cuisine, beadwork, and fiddle. Follow the "Learn More" links under each heading.
∞ Link: Symbols of Métis Culture
lessons.teacherNotes:
• IMPORTANT DATES
First settlers arrive at the Selkirk settlement in Red River (1811)

links.text: Map showing the Selkirk Land Grant in 1811

Rebellion and Reform
⍞ Materials: The Story of Canada

➜ RECAP
What did you read about immigration to Canada in the early nineteenth century?

➜ INTRO
How do we choose our government in Canada and in our provinces and territories? Do you think it has always been this way in Canada? Today we will learn about frustrations towards the government in the 1830s, which would eventually lead to the form of government we recognize today.

➜ VIEW
Remember that in 1791, the large Province of Quebec was divided into Upper and Lower Canada, roughly corresponding to the provinces of Ontario and Quebec today.
∞ Link: Upper & Lower Canada in 1791

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.93 "Bytown and Ottawa"
p.94-97 "In the 1830s" - "disguised in women's clothing"

• What changes did William Lyon Mackenzie want to see in Upper Canada?

lessons.title: 25m World History: Grades 4-6 - Lesson 25
lessons.body:
Aliens Arrive
⍞ Materials: Commodore Perry in the Land of the Shogun, Student World Atlas

➜INTRO
This is a non-fiction book that tells the story of how U.S. Navy ships headed by Commodore Matthew Perry ships sailed to Japan in 1853 to bring a letter from President Fillmore asking Japan to open their ports to trading with the U.S. Other attempts had been tried before but were unsuccessful.  Due to policies in Japan, they had remained isolated from most other countries for hundreds of years. Imagine you are living in your small fishing village of Shimoda and had never met anyone from another country. You see four huge ships approaching. That is what it was like for the people on that day in July 8, 1853. Let's first learn about the geography of Japan. 

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: Student World Atlas p.30-31, 92-92
1.On which continent is Japan found? (Asia)
2.How would you get from the U.S. to Japan in a ship if you were going to leave from the east coast? ( Sail east across the Atlantic Ocean, around the southern tip of Africa, and across the Indian Ocean and western Pacific.)
3.Which countries are closest to Japan? (North and South Korea, Russia, China)
4.Is Japan north or south of the equator? (north)
5.What is the capital of Japan? (Tokyo)
6.Turn to p.91-92. How many main islands is Japan made up of? (4) Name them. (Hokkaido, Honshu, Shikoku, Kyushu) There are also nearly 4,000 smaller islands!
7.Which island is the largest? (Honshu)
8.What bodies of water surround Japan? (the Pacific Ocean, Sea of Japan / East Sea, East China Sea, Sea of Okhotsk)
9.What physical features cover much of Japan? (Almost four-fifths of Japan is covered with mountains.)
10.Why might Japan’s island location have helped protect it from invasions?


➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.13-20 Beginning-"ship to leave."
• Why do you think the title of the first chapter is "Aliens Arrive"?
• Why might they think American ships are not coming on friendly terms?

➜ VIEW
1.∞ Image Links: Chart of Feudal System & Japanese Society
Japan was still based on a feudal society which means society was divided into strict social classes where everyone had a specific role that had to follow. Read the description of Japanese society and look at the chart that shows the feudal system and who has the most power and the least power. 
2.Look at the illustrations at the beginning of the book and in ch.1 Then read about the illustrations on p.138. Most of the illustrations come that time period from either Japanese artists or American artists that were part of the expedition.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• DEFINITIONS
turmoil: a state or condition of extreme confusion (p.14)

inept:(p.15) lacking qualities (as knowledge, skill, or ability) required to do a job

lessons.title: 25m Ancient History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 25
lessons.body:
The Ancient Church; Peter
⍞ Materials: Redemption: The Church in Ancient Times, Historical Atlas of the World

➜ INTRO
In the Bronze Bow, you read a historical fiction book about what was happening in the land of Israel during the time of Jesus around 30 AD. This book tells true accounts of the Christians who lived in the 1st century and carried on the message of Jesus after he ascended into heaven. The events in this book take place from 30 AD to 350 AD. This was still a time when the Romans controlled all the areas around the Mediterranean Sea. All the people you will meet lived in Roman occupied territories. Let's first look at a map to understand what the Roman Empire looked like at this time. 

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: Historical Atlas of the World p.16-17
1.Name all the bodies of water that surround the Roman Empire, (Atlantic Ocean, Cantabrian Sea, North Sea,Mediterranean Sea, Ligurian Sea, Tyrrhenian Sea, Adriatic Sea,Ionina Sea, Aegean Sea, Adriatic Sea, Black Sea, Red Sea)
2.What country was furthest north in the Roman Empire? (Britain)
3.What walls were built near the northernmost part of the Roman Empire? (Wall of Hadrian and Wall of Antoninus)
4.What country was furthest south in the Roman Republic? (Egypt)
5.What country was furthest west in the Roman Republic? (Spain)
6.What country was furthest east in the Roman Republic? (Osroene)
7.What two rivers in the north formed the borders between the Roman Empire and Germany? (Rhine and Danube)
8.Find Jerusalem. That is where the apostles were after the ascension of Jesus.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
1.p.11-12 "At not point" - "was increasingly marginalized." Then last paragraph on p. 13 "The story of.."

2.We will read about the apostle Peter. What do you remember about him? (He cut off the ear of a soldier when Jesus was arrested in the Garden of Gethsemane, he denied Jesus three times) The event we will read about is told in the Bible in Acts 2.   Forty days after the resurrection and tens days after Jesus ascended to heaven, about 120 followers of Christ gathered together in Jerusalem to celebrate a Jewish feast that had been celebrated since the Old Testament.
 p. 17-21 "Peter took the" - "And prayed."
lessons.teacherNotes:
• DEFINITIONS
frayed: fragile or uncertain (p.11)

ambassador: representative or messenger (p.11)

marginalized: given an unimportant or powerless position within a society or group (p.12)

zealot: member of a first-century Jewish political movement that fiercely opposed the Roman occupation of Judea (p.20)

lessons.title: 25m World History: Grades 4-6 - Lesson 26
lessons.body:
The Black Ships of Evil Men; His High and Mighty Mysteriousness
⍞ Materials: Commodore Perry in the Land of the Shogun

➜ RECAP
What did you read last time?

➜ INTRO
The four Americans ships full of 560 men  of the U.S. Navy have arrived off the coast of Japan and now we will find out how the Japanese will respond. What do you think they will do?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.20-29
• Why do you think Commodore Perry stood firm on his decision to not sail to the port in Nagasaki or meet with lower officials? (Interesting note: It was said that Perry realized that previous failures had resulted from insufficient show of strength and ignorance of Japanese character, two mistakes he would never make.)
• What are the some of the differences you notice so far between the Americans and Japanese?
lessons.teacherNotes: moored: anchored (p.20)

lessons.title: 25m Ancient History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 26
lessons.body:
Peter; begin Paul
⍞ Materials: Redemption: The Church in Ancient Times

➜RECAP
What did you read last time?

➜ INTRO
You are reading parts of two chapters today. You will finish the chapter about Peter and find out what happened on the special event we call "The Church's Birthday." Then you will start the section on Paul who was not one of the original twelve apostles of Jesus but was used mightily by God to spread the gospel message. 

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
1.p.21- 25 "Peter did not know"-end
• Why is this event know as the church's birthday?

➜ VIEW
∞ Image Link: Pentecost by Giotto
Many artists have painted scenes of the Holy Spirit coming at Pentecost. Take a moment to look at this painting from Giotto painted around 1310-18. What is the centerpiece of the painting? (dove) What do you notice around the heads of the apostles? Artists traditionally put halos or a glowing rings of light to show a holy or sacred figure. You also notice the "tongues of fire." Do you see the two men outside of the doors? Giotto painted the scene using a low wall to divide those experiencing the event and those straining to hear what was going on.

2.Now we meet the apostle Paul whose story is also told in the Bible. He wrote 13 of the 27 books of the New Testament. His writings were initially letters or epistles written to various churches. The story we are reading today can be found in Acts 16.
p.29-31 "This way!" - "but little comfort."
lessons.teacherNotes:
• DEFINITIONS
gale: a strong current of air (p.21)

Shavuot: Jewish holiday (p.23)

lunacy: insanity, madness (p.29)

orator: (p.29)

lessons.title: 25m Ancient History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 28
lessons.body:
Persecution of the Church
⍞ Materials: Redemption: The Church in Ancient Times

NOTE: We are skipping Fact Files p.39-42 and reading this PDF instead.

➜RECAP
What did you read last time?

➜ INTRO
You will read a chapter about why the Romans despised the Christians and how that led to persecution and the killing of Christians.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ PDF Link: Trial by Fire (from The Church of Our Fathers by Roland Bainton) Skip the section about Polycarp p.23-24 "There was a" - "through Jesus Christ."

• What made Christianity so different than the religions of Rome?
• Why do you think so many Christians were willing to die for their faith?
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER NOTE
Preview p.22-23 that talk about Nero's treatment of Christians and the accusation that Christians were cannibals. It may not be appropriate for sensitive students.

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 29
lessons.body:
The Loxleys and Confederation
⍞ Materials: The Loxleys and Confederation

➜ INTRO
For the next four weeks, you will read an account of the years leading up to Confederation through the eyes of a fictional family, the Loxleys, in the graphic novel The Loxleys and Confederation. Look for historical figures and places (and a piece of artwork!) that you have been learning about this year. 

➜ READ & NARRATE
Beginning with the Prologue on page 9, read for about 20 minutes, leaving time for narration. This should allow enough time to read through The Loxleys and Confederation over four weeks. Since the time to read graphic novels varies, some students will also have time to read Lillian's 1867 Diary at the end, while others can save that for afternoon occupations or an extra helping.
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER NOTE
Parents/teachers may wish to preview the several images of battles, which might be too vivid for sensitive students.

lessons.title: 25m Ancient History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 29
lessons.body:
Ignatius
⍞ Materials: Redemption: The Church in Ancient Times, The Student Bible Atlas

➜RECAP
What did you read last time?

➜ LOCATE
⍞ Map Resource: Student Bible Atlas p.20-21
Find Antioch on the east side of the Mediterranean Sea. Why do you think that location would be a good place for trade and the spread of Christianity? Antioch was one of the largest cities in the first-century Roman world, accommodating a population between 100,000 and 300,000. There was a thriving church there made up of Jews and Gentiles and Paul had stayed there for a whole year ministering to the people. (Acts 11) Trace a sea route to Rome that stays close to the coast. 

➜ INTRO
We will read about Ignatius  who was bishop of Antioch (modern-day Turkey) for forty years, probably from AD 67–106.  At this time, it was the policy of the Roman government to not to seek out Christians, but still to punish them when they were brought before the authorities. "Once accused and brought before the authorities, Christians had to be forced to worship the gods of the empire, or face punishment...Since great festivities were being planned in Rome in celebration of a military victory, Ignatius was sent to the capital so that his death might provide entertainment for the people. On his way to martyrdom, he wrote seven letters that are among the most valuable documents informing our knowledge of early Christianity" (Justo Gonzalez, The Story of Christianity).

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.45-51
• Why do you think he had such courage to face his death?

➜ VIEW
∞ Image Links: 
1.Fresco of Ignatius of Antioch 
2.Inside view of the Colosseum where many Christian were martyred.

lessons.title: 25m Ancient History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 30
lessons.body:
Polycarp
⍞ Materials: Redemption: The Church in Ancient Times, Historical Atlas of the World

➜ RECAP
What did you read last time?

➜ LOCATE
⍞ Map Resource: Historical Atlas of the World p.16-17
Find Smyrna (E5) Where is it located? (In Asia Minor on the coast of the Aegean Sea. 

➜ INTRO
We will read about another bishop and martyr of the second century. His name was Polycarp which means 'much fruit' in Greek. Many years earlier than when this account takes place,  Ignatius of Antioch had advised young Bishop Polycarp regarding his duties as bishop and the need to be firm in his faith. You may remember that Igantius wrote 7 letters on his way to Rome. One of those letters was to Polycarp. We will read how Polycarp showed himself a worthy recipient of Ignatius’s advice, and a follower of his example.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.53-60
• Did you notice what the Romans called Polycarp? (Roman persecutors called the Christians "atheists" because they refused to worship the Roman gods.)Why is that odd to call him that? 
• What do you think about Polycarp's last speech? What does it tell us about his faith?

➜ VIEW
∞ Image Link: Fresco of Polycarp

lessons.title: 25m Ancient History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 31
lessons.body:
Cyprian
⍞ Materials: Redemption: The Church in Ancient Times

➜RECAP
What did you read last time?

➜ LOCATE
⍞ Map Resource: Historical Atlas of the World p.16-17
Find Carthage (F7) Where is it located? (In Africa on the Mediterranean Sea.)

➜ INTRO
Cyprian was the bishop of the church in Carthage from 248 to 258 AD. His writings and teachers have been beneficial to Christians. He helped Christians navigate the question: Do we let people who denied Christ under the threat of torture and death back into the church if they repent? 

➜ BACKGROUND
In AD 250 the most violent persecution the church had yet faced was instigated by the emperor Decius (249–51). Decius was determined to have no nonsense from Christians. In his eyes, they were enemies of the empire. Their atheism was responsible for the many troubles in the realm. Thus Decius commanded all citizens of the empire to sacrifice to the traditional Roman gods. Those who did so were given certificates (libelli in Latin) as evidence that they had obeyed the order. Those who refused to comply and were unable (or unwilling) to obtain false libelli from sympathetic or corrupt officials faced death. To save their lives, many Christians complied. Others were able to obtain certificates without having actually sacrificed. But an unknown number of Christians were imprisoned or executed—among them the bishops of Rome, Antioch, and Jerusalem. (Church History in Plain Language)

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.63-71

• How important is it for Christians to be part of a church body and to attend church regularly? What do you think Cyprian was saying about that topic?
• Why do you think there was disagreement on whether those who denied their faith should be let back in the church?

➜ VIEW
This is a mosaic that is on the wall in a church in Ravenna, Italy that was constructed in the 6th century. A mosaic is a pattern or image made of small regular or irregular pieces of colored stone or glass held in place by plaster and covering a surface. Mosaics are often used as floor and wall decoration, and were particularly popular in the ancient Roman world. On this wall is a procession of martyrs walking from the king's palace towards Christ's throne in heaven. In the other image you can the inside of the church. Polycarp was also represented in this mosaic. 
∞ Image Links: Mosaic of Cyprian, Inside of

lessons.title: 25m Canadian History: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 33
lessons.body:
Confederation
⍞ Materials: The Story of Canada

➜ INTRO
In 1867, Canada looked very different from what it does today. It consisted of four provinces: Nova Scotia, New Brunswick, Quebec, and Ontario. How and when did other provinces join Confederation? And what did this mean for the Indigenous peoples in the west and north who had lived on the land for centuries?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.166-172 "July 1, 1867" - "Riel was an exile."

• Why did some colonies choose not to join in 1867? 
• What were some effects of Canada's purchase of Rupert's Land from the Hudson's Bay Company?

➜ VIEW
Look at the map of Rupert's Land, the land sold to Canada by the Hudson's Bay Company in 1869. Which provinces and territories now exist on land that was once part of Rupert's Land?
∞ Map Link: Rupert's Land
lessons.teacherNotes:
• EXTRA HELPING
Listen to a performance of the Anti-Confederation Song, recorded only ten years after Newfoundland eventually joined Canada in 1949.
∞ Music Link: Alan Mills - Anti-Confederation Song (1959)

• IMPORTANT DATES
Louis Riel (1844-1885)
Manitoba joins Canada (1870)

lessons.teacherNotes:
• EXTRA HELPING
Louis Riel was tried and hanged for high treason in 1885, but history has come to regard him as, instead, one of the fathers of Confederation - the father of Manitoba. In 2024, Manitoba formally acknowledged him as their first Premier.
∞ Article Link: Riel formally recognized as first Premier

Did you notice the young boy front and centre in the photograph of the last spike being hammered into the railway at Craigellachie, BC? Learn more about him.
∞ Article Link: The Kid in the Last Spike

John A. Macdonald's wife Agnes had a unique way of riding the train! See another photograph of her from 1887.
∞ Article Link: History Corner -1887- Lady Agnes Macdonald

View a promotional poster from 1885 advertising the railroad and farming opportunities on the prairies.
∞ Image Link: The Canadian Pacific Railroad

terms.content:
Canadian History: Grades 5-6
· (Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune) Describe what life was like in Herring Cove, Nova Scotia, around 1800; consider historical details such as food, shelter, clothing, transportation, medical care, family life, communication, etc.

Canadian History: Grades 5-6
· (The Story of Canada and Tecumseh ) Describe Métis culture in the West: who are they, how did they live, and what was life like for them in the first decades of the nineteenth century. OR Explain who Tecumseh was and his role in the War of 1812.

Canadian History: Grades 5-6
· (The Story of Canada and The Loxleys and Confederation) Tell all you know about the events leading to Canada's Confederation on July 1, 1867. OR Explain why it was so important for Canada to have a transcontinental railway.
